title: Timers and Smart Relays
course: Second Period - Magnetic Controls and Switching Circuits
publisher: ILM Learning Resources for Skilled Trades

pages.content:
Timers and Smart Relays

Rationale
Why is it important for you to learn this skill?
Electrical circuits are designed to control a wide variety of operations. Some of these circuits require events to occur after a specific time has elapsed. With technology improving and the world becoming increasingly automated, it is important that you are aware of and understand some of the equipment that can provide timing control.

Outcome
When you have completed this module, you will be able to
Analyze timers and smart relays.

Objectives
1. Describe timers and basic timing functions.
2. Describe smart relays and basic timing functions.
3. Connect and analyze timers and smart relays.

NOTE: Objective Three is a practical objective and is not covered in this module.

Introduction
This module describes different timing devices with emphasis on timing relays. Different timing functions and some timing relay control circuits are illustrated. The basic principles of smart relays are also examined.

Objective One
When you have completed this objective, you will be able to
Describe timers and basic timing functions.

Timers
Timers were developed to provide basic automatic control of a system. Timers can allow a motor to start and run for a certain period and then stop automatically, or they can allow a motor to start automatically after a period of time. As technology has improved, timers have become more sophisticated and accurate.

The three basic types of timing devices are:
- Spring-wound interval timers,
- Mechanical and electronic time switches, and
- Timing relays.

Spring-Wound Interval Timers
Spring-wound interval timers are designed to be used as wall switches and provide a timed-on interval. These are commonly used for lights, fans, bathroom spa motors, and sauna heaters. As the dial is turned to the desired position, a spring tightens. When the dial is released, the spring drives a clock mechanism to the point, a spring tightens. When the stays on for 15 minutes or 16 minutes is not important. For these applications, whether the timer time, allowing the controlled load to stay connected. Although relatively inexpensive, they are not particularly accurate for the set time.

Mechanical and Electronic Time Switches
Mechanical time switches use small electrically driven clock motors that control adjustable trip mechanisms or cams to change the state of electrical contacts. Electronic time switches, such as the one shown in Figure 1, use an electronic clock to control electrical contacts.

Figure 1 - Electronic time switch.

figures: Figure 2 - Electronic timing relays (Panel-Mount, Plug-In, Rail-Mounted, Dial-Adjusted)
pages.content:
Timing Relays
Both mechanical and electronic time switches are available to control a wide range of currents. They can control circuits over a 24-hour or seven-day timing cycle. These timers are often found in systems that control security lighting, landscape, irrigation, and parking timers. They are not particularly accurate, whereas mechanical timers cost less but are not particularly accurate.

Although spring-wound interval timers and electronic time switches are reliable, inexpensive, and commonly used, they are not suitable for all applications. Some motor control applications require the precision and versatility that timing relays provide.

Timing relays can have their timed operation controlled pneumatically (that is, by air), by a pneumatic and fluid-filled dashpot (usually a constant viscosity-controlled pneumatically oil), or by electronic circuits. Timing is adjusted by varying the size of hole or orifice. Although these types of timers are still in use, generally they have been replaced with electronic timing relays that are more accurate, versatile, smaller, and cost-effective. With electronic timing relays that are more accurate, versatile, smaller, and cost-effective.

In this module, you will see NO (normally open) and NC (normally closed). In industry, this may be expressed in multiple ways:
- Normally open: N.O., NO, and NOC
- Normally closed: N.C., NC, and NCC

Figure 2 - Electronic timing relays. Timing is adjusted by connecting the appropriate resistors to the terminals.

pages.section: Objective One - Basic Timing Functions / Time Delay on Energization (TDOE)
pages.content:
Basic Timing Functions
There are five basic timing functions provided by timing relays:
- Time delay on energization,
- Time delay on de-energization,
- One-shot timing, and
- Interval timing,
- Repeat cycle timing.

It is important to understand the differences between these functions to select the correct timing relay to meet the requirements of the installation.

Time Delay on Energization
Time delay on energization (TDOE) contacts change state after a specified time measured from the instant the relay coil is energized, as shown in Figure 3. When the relay coil is de-energized, the contacts revert to their normal state immediately. This type of timer can also be referred to as on-delay or delay-on-operation.

Figure 3 - TDOE timing function.
Contact Position: Timed Contact, Normally Open, Normally Closed
Time Delay: NC stay closed / NO stay open (timing)
Coil Energized: Timed contacts have changed their position / NC close / NO open
All contacts are back in their normal position.

Figure 4 - TDOE circuit.
Analyze the schematic diagram in Figure 4. When the start button is pressed, Motor 1 (M1) starts and runs for three minutes, then Motor 2 (M2) automatically starts, and both motors continue to run until the stop button is pressed. An example of this process is a system where a lubrication pump must run for three minutes to build up heating pressure before the main motor can start.

pages.section: Objective One - Time Delay on De-Energization (TDOD)
pages.content:
Time Delay on De-Energization
Time delay on de-energization (TDOD) contacts change state instantly when a relay coil is energized. However, they only revert to their normal state after a specified time measured from the instant the relay coil is de-energized, as shown in Figure 5. This type of timer can also be referred to as off-delay.

Figure 5 - TDOD timing function.
Contact Position: Timed Contact, Normally Open, Normally Closed
Coil Energized: Timed contacts change their position / NC open / NO close
Coil De-Energized: Timed contacts remain changed / NC stay open / NO stay closed (Time Delay)
All contacts are back in their normal position / NC close / NO open.

Figure 6 - TDOD circuit.
Analyze the schematic diagram illustrated in Figure 6. When the start button is pressed, both Motor 1 (M1) and Motor 2 (M2) start and run at the same time. When the stop button is pressed, Motor 1 stops but Motor 2 continues to run for 5 minutes before stopping. A mandatory emergency stop button must be used when TDOD contacts are in the circuit since pressing the emergency stop button immediately stops both motors.

pages.section: Objective One - Comparison of TDOE and TDOD / Interval Timing / One-Shot Timing
figures: Figure 7 - Timer contact operation (TDOE and TDOD comparison table)
pages.content:
Comparison of TDOE and TDOD Timer Contacts
Figure 7 is a comparison of contact operation for TDOE and TDOD timing relays.

TDOE Contacts:
- Coil is de-energized: Contacts are in their normal condition.
- Coil is instant energized: Contacts will not change for a period of time (timing starts).
- Coil has been energized long enough to happen (timed out): Contacts change effect - (timed out). Contacts will change back to their normal condition.

TDOD Contacts:
- Coil is de-energized: Contacts are instanty change condition.
- Coil is instant energized: Contacts will not change. Contacts are in their normal condition.
- Coil has been energized long enough: Contacts will change (timed out - de-energized). Contacts change back to normal (timed - lined out).

Interval Timing
Interval timing contacts change state instantly when a relay coil is energized but will revert to their normal state after a specified time. The time is measured from the instant the relay coil is energized, even if the relay coil remains energized for a longer period.

The sequence of operation of interval timing is illustrated in Figure 8.

Figure 8 - Interval timing function.
Coil Power: ON
Timed Contact Output: CHANGED - Timed contacts have changed condition, NO are closed, NC are open.
NORMAL - Timed contacts are back to normal condition, NO are open, NC are closed.

An example application of interval timing is to control an exhaust fan used to purge the combustion chamber in a furnace before a gas valve opens and an ignition sequence begins. The fan blows fresh air through the system to prevent the explosion that could occur if large amounts of residual gas were allowed to build up. The exhaust fan runs for a pre-set period each time the heating cycle begins.

One-Shot Timing
The relay coil of a one-shot timing relay requires an extra set of terminals to receive a start or initiating signal. The relay coil is energized continuously, but the timed contacts do not change states until the relay terminals see a signal. The start signal could be activated manually with a push button or automatically with a pilot device such as a limit switch or pressure switch. Once the start signal is received, the contacts change state, and the timing function begins. After the specified time, the contacts revert to their normal state and remain in that state until the next time the initiating terminals see a start signal.

The sequence of operation of one-shot timing is depicted in Figure 9.

Figure 9 - One-shot timing function.
Coil Power: ON
Start Signal: OFF / ON
Timed Contact Output: CHANGED - Timed contacts have changed condition, NO are closed, NC are open.
NORMAL - Timed contacts are back to normal condition, NO are open, NC are closed.

An example application of one-shot timing is to run an oil-spray pump on a machine tool such as a lathe or drill-press. While the machine is operating, the oil-spray button is pressed to provide lubrication for a set period of time.

pages.section: Objective One - Repeat Cycle Timing / Multi-Function Timing Relays
figures: Figure 10 - Repeat cycle timing function: cycle started with contacts in the changed condition
Figure 11 - Repeat cycle timing function: cycle started with contacts in the normal condition
Figure 12 - Multi-function timing relay with basic timing functions
Figure 13 - Multi-function timing relay with additional timing functions
pages.content:
Repeat Cycle Timing
Repeat cycle timing provides a regular on-and-off cycle for the timed contacts. The on/off cycle may be started by energizing the relay coil or by a signal to a start terminal, similar to one-shot timing. For typical repeat cycle operation, the contacts change state, and the timing function until the relay coil is de-energized, as shown in Figure 10.

Some repeat cycle timing relays can provide an alternative sequence. In these instances, the timing function begins when the relay coil is energized, but the contacts remain in the normal state until the end of the first timing sequence, as shown in Figure 11.

Figure 10 - Repeat cycle timing function; cycle started with contacts in the changed condition.
Figure 11 - Repeat cycle timing function; cycle started with contacts in the normal condition.

An example application of repeat cycle timing is an air exhaust system in a room designed to provide air changes at specific intervals. Instead of operating the exhaust system continually, some multi-function timing relays offer separately selectable on time and off time; this might look something like 5 minutes on, 2 minutes off, then repeat.

Multi-Function Timing Relays
Multi-function electronic timing relays can provide several different timing functions. Some multi-function relays, such as the one in Figure 12, only provide the basic timing functions. Some multi-function relays offer additional timing functions, depending on the manufacturer, as shown on the multi-function timing relay in Figure 13.

Figure 12 - Multi-function timing relay with basic timing functions.
SETTING: L-TIME, RANGE
FUNCTION: A - ON DELAY, B/G - INTERVAL, C/P/I - OFF DELAY, E or K - REPEAT CYCLE

Figure 13 - Multi-function timing relay with additional timing functions.
FUNCTION: A - ON DELAY, B - FLASHER-ON 1ST, C - OFF DELAY, D - FLASHER-OFF 1ST, F - FLASHER-ON 1ST, H - SHOT FALLING EDGE, J - WATCHDOG, K - TRIG ON DELAY
TIME RANGE

Reset Terminals
Some multi-function timing relays may offer a reset terminal as well as a start terminal. This allows the timing sequence to be reset before it has completed. The reset signal could be activated by manual pilot devices or by automatic pilot devices.

pages.section: Objective One - Multi-Function Timing Relays (continued) / Reset Terminals
pages.content:
Reset Terminals (continued)
Some multi-function timing relays may offer a reset terminal as well as a start terminal. This allows the timing sequence to be reset before it has completed. The reset signal could be activated by manual pilot devices or by automatic pilot devices.

Objective One Activity
1. Name three (3) basic types of timing devices used in electrical circuits.
   a) One-shot
   b) Interval
   c) Repeat

2. Describe three (3) methods of controlling the timing of a timing relay.
   a) Pneumatic
   b) Fluid-filled dashpot
   c) Electronic relay

3. What does TDOE mean? Time delay on energization

4. What does TDOD mean? Time delay on de-energization

5. When a TDOE timing relay is first energized, would the condition of the following be open or closed?
   a) An NO timed contact: Open
   b) An NC timed contact: Close
   c) An NO, IC contact: Open

6. When a TDOD timing relay is first energized, would the condition of the following be open or closed?
   a) An NO timed contact: Open (close)
   b) An NC timed contact: Closed (open)
   c) An NC, IC contact: Open

7. When a TDOE timing relay has timed out, would the condition of the following be open or closed?
   a) An NO timed contact: Closed
   b) An NC timed contact: Open
   c) An NO, IC contact: Closed

8. When a TDOD timing relay has just been de-energized, would the condition of the following be open or closed?
   a) An NO timed contact: Closed
   b) An NC timed contact: Open
   c) An NO, IC contact: Open
   d) An NC, IC contact: Closed

9. Name three (3) additional basic functions that may be provided by a multi-function timing relay.
   a) Time
   b) Function
   c) Time range

10. When a multi-function timing relay that is set for interval timing has timed out, would the condition of the following be open or closed?
    a) An NO timed contact: Open
    b) An NC timed contact: Closed

11. When a multi-function timing relay is set for one-shot timing, how is the timing period initiated?
    A signal at the start/initiating terminals

12. Briefly describe a repeat cycle timing function.
    This is when one function is engaged for a certain period of time, then the other turns on, again for a certain period of time, then turns off again. Symmetrical is same off time; non-symmetrical is the opposite.

Objective Two
When you have completed this objective, you will be able to
Describe smart relays and basic timing functions.

Smart Relays
As the size and complexity of control systems grew, the number of relays and space requirements to install these control systems became too large. Individual timing relays are fine for a control system that requires a handful of timing functions; however, for a complex control system requiring hundreds or thousands of timing functions, individual relays are not practical. This problem was solved with the introduction of the programmable logic controller (PLC). Smart relays evolved out of PLC technology to provide the simplicity of traditional relays with the programmability and intelligence of PLCs. Smart relays are commonly used in situations where full-scale PLCs are overkill in terms of complexity and cost.

Smart relays, such as the one shown in Figure 14, are simply relays and/or timers that are programmable. They can have numerous inputs and outputs along with some memory to store a program.

Figure 14 - Smart relay.

One of the key features of smart relays is their programmability. Users can define logic, control, and timing sequences with a user-friendly graphical interface, often through ladder logic or function block diagrams. Programming can be done using schematic diagrams, and function block diagrams look similar to block diagrams. These programming languages are covered in more detail in the later years of your technical training.

The required features include:
- The number of inputs and outputs and voltage ratings,
- Whether there is a keypad and/or digital display, and
- Whether there is wireless communication functionality.

pages.section: Objective Two - Smart Relay Functions / Table 1
pages.content:
Smart Relay Functions
Unlike traditional relays, which require rewiring to change their functionality, smart relays can be easily reprogrammed to adapt to changing requirements. Their ability to integrate multiple functions into a single device makes them suitable for tasks that require a combination of relay-based control, timers, counters, and communication with other devices.

A few examples of common smart relay functions are included in Table 1.

Table 1 - Smart relay functions.

Relay Function / Description:
- TDOE Timing: Changes the normal condition of the contacts once the set time has elapsed after an input signal
- TDOD Timing: Changes the normal condition of the contacts immediately also elapsed after the input signal is removed
- One-Shot Timer (or Triggered): Cycles the contacts between the normal and changed states each time the start terminal receives a signal
- Repetitive Timing; Cycling Timing: Cycles the contacts between the normal and changed states after an input signal (on and off times are the same) or by a non-symmetrical (different on and off times). The timing can also be programmed to be initiated by a maintained input signal or by a momentary input signal.
- Daily/Weekly/Monthly Timing Function: Cycles the contacts between the normal and changed states based on a schedule. The timing function can operate various scales, such as days, weeks, or months. Smart relay is sometimes referred to as real-time clock scheduling. Several start and end times can be programmed for each scale. This could be compared to the application and the capabilities for each scale.
- Totalizing Time with Reset: Provides the option of totaling the time for a sequence of operations, then restarting before the sequence is repeated. This could be compared to the operation of a stopwatch.

pages.section: Objective Two - Smart Relay Applications / Table 2
pages.content:
Smart Relay Applications
Smart relays are particularly valuable in applications such as building automation, lighting control, heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems, and small-scale manufacturing, where they streamline processes and enhance energy efficiency. Smart relays can control when something starts, how long it runs, when it stops, and which day of the week the action occurs. Some examples are provided in Table 2.

Table 2 - Smart relay application examples.

Application / Operation Details:
- Small Machinery Automation: Automatic control for a car wash where water is turned on, brushes start, then more water and other is turned on, brushes start and turn off, and during certain times of the day, or control of automatic door openers where the door is only permitted to operate during certain times of the day, or control of a door and operation of warning lights that a door is in use.
- Lighting Management: Turning building lights on or off at a specified time each night and on weekends.
- Access, Control, and Surveillance Management: Smart relays are used to allow access only during parts of a day or days of a week. Control of surveillance cameras can also be controlled using smart relays, operating certain cameras at certain times.

Objective Two Activity
1. What programming languages are smart relays typically programmed with?
   PLC and programming logic controller

2. What are smart relays often compared to?
   Small programmable logic controllers (PLCs)

3. Name four (4) applications for smart relays.
   a) Small machinery automation
   b) Lighting management
   c) Access control management
   d) Surveillance automation

4. List four (4) of the timer functions smart relays are capable of.
   a) Relays
   b) TDOE timing
   c) TDOD timing
   d) Surveillance management automation

5. What is the difference between symmetrical and non-symmetrical cycle timing?
   Symmetrical is same off time; non-symmetrical is the opposite.

Self-Test
1. Which type of timer is best suited for a bathroom fan?
   a) Spring wound interval timer
   b) Timing relay
   c) Mechanical time clock
   d) Repeat cycle timer

2. Which timing relay function always requires a start signal?
   a) Interval
   b) Off-delay
   c) On-delay
   d) One-shot

3. Which statement best describes the normally open (NO) contacts of a TDOE timing relay at the instant the relay coil is energized?
   a) Closed, not timing
   b) Closed, timing to open
   c) Open, timing to close
   d) Open, not timing

4. Which statement best describes the normally open (NO) contacts of a TDOD timing relay at the instant the relay coil is energized?
   a) Open, not timing
   b) Open, timing to close
   c) Closed, timing to open
   d) Closed, not timing

5. An interval timing relay is set for 5 seconds. If the relay coil for this timer has been energized for 12 seconds, what must happen for the contacts to change state again?
   a) An NO timed contact must be de-energized.
   b) The relay coil must be de-energized.
   c) The contacts will change states in three more seconds.
   d) The relay coil must be de-energized and then re-energized.

6. What is the difference between a smart relay and a timing relay?
   a) A smart relay has overloads, but a timing relay does not.
   b) There is no difference between a smart relay and a timing relay.
   c) Smart relays can be programmed to provide many timing functions.
   d) Smart relays are rated for higher voltages and currents than timing relays.

7. In a smart relay, which timing function would be used to delay an action for a set period of time after an input signal is received?
   a) Cycling timing
   b) Interval timing
   c) On-delay timing
   d) Off-delay timing

Objective One Activity Answers
1. a) Spring wound interval timers
   b) Mechanical and electronic time switches
   c) Timing relays

2. a) Pneumatically
   b) Fluid dash pot
   c) Electronically

3. Time delay on energization
4. Time delay on de-energization
5. a) An NO timed contact: open
   b) An NC timed contact: closed
   c) An NO, IC contact: open

6. a) An NO timed contact: closed
   b) An NC timed contact: open
   c) An NO, IC contact: closed
   d) An NC, IC contact: open

7. a) An NO timed contact: closed
   b) An NC timed contact: open
   c) An NO, IC contact: open

8. a) An NO timed contact: closed
   b) An NC timed contact: open
   c) An NO, IC contact: open
   d) An NC, IC contact: closed

9. a) Interval timing
   b) One-shot timing
   c) Repeat cycle timing

10. a) An NO timed contact: open
    b) An NC timed contact: closed

11. A signal at the start/initiating terminals

12. Repeat cycle timing alternates the contacts between the normal and changed states for a set period of time in each state. The cycles repeat until the relay coil is de-energized.

Objective Two Activity Answers
1. Function block and ladder logic
2. Small programmable logic controllers (PLCs)
3. Any four (4) of the following answers are correct:
   a) Small machinery automation
   b) Decentralized automation systems supplementary to medium and large machinery
   c) Lighting management
   d) Access, control, and surveillance management
   e) Heating and air conditioning management
   f) Automatic control and sequencing for car washes
   g) Automatic door control

4. Any four (4) of the following answers are correct:
   a) On-delay
   b) Off-delay
   c) One-shot
   d) Repeat cycle with a maintained input
   e) Repeat cycle with a momentary input
   f) Symmetrical or non-symmetrical cycle timing
   g) Real-time clock scheduling
   h) Totalizing timer with reset

5. For symmetrical cycle timing, the timing for the normal and changed states of the contacts is the same. For non-symmetrical cycle timing, the normal state time and changed state time are different.

Self-Test Answers
1. a) Spring wound interval timer
2. d) One-shot
3. c) Open, timing to close
4. b) Closed, not timing
5. d) The relay coil must be de-energized and then re-energized.
6. c) Smart relays can be programmed to provide many timing functions.
7. d) On-delay timing
